v.t: Construction of Göbekli Tepe
v.s: Around 9600 BCE, construction began on Göbekli Tepe in southeastern Anatolia (modern-day Şanlıurfa, Turkey). The site consists of multiple circular megalithic enclosures with T-shaped limestone pillars about 3-6 meters tall, weighing 10-20 tons, carved with animal reliefs (foxes, snakes, wild boars, birds, etc.). Göbekli Tepe is the world's oldest known monumental ritual complex, predating the agricultural revolution and sedentary life. Its discovery fundamentally changed understanding of the Neolithic Revolution. The site was abandoned and deliberately buried around 8000 BCE.

v.t: Jericho becomes earliest large settlement
v.s: Around 9000 BCE, Jericho in the Levant (modern-day Palestine) developed into one of the earliest known large settlements, covering about 4 hectares with a population of about 2000. Residents practiced early agriculture (growing barley and wheat) and trade (importing obsidian from Anatolia). Around 8300-8000 BCE, they built a stone defensive wall about 2 meters thick and a round stone tower about 8.5 meters high, indicating that early social organization had the capacity for collective labor and defense.

v.t: Construction of Nevalı Çori temple complex
v.s: Around 8400 BCE, the construction of monumental buildings began at Nevalı Çori in southeastern Anatolia (Şanlıurfa Province, Turkey). Contemporaneous with Göbekli Tepe, the site yielded T-shaped pillars and giant stone human statues (about 2 meters high), with pillars carved with animal motifs such as snakes and scorpions. Evidence of early agriculture (cultivation of einkorn wheat) was also found, suggesting that ritual activities were closely linked to early farming communities.

v.t: Production of 'Ain Ghazal statues
v.s: Around 8200-7500 BCE, the site of 'Ain Ghazal (near modern Amman, Jordan) in the Levant produced several life-sized humanoid plaster statues, now housed in the Amman Museum. These are among the earliest known large-scale human statues, with eyes outlined in bitumen and plaster bodies over reed cores. The site covers about 15 hectares, with a population of about 3000, making it one of the largest Pre-Pottery Neolithic C settlements, engaged in agriculture and trade.

v.t: Rice domestication at Pengtoushan culture in China
v.s: Around 8000-7500 BCE, the Pengtoushan culture (modern Li County, Hunan) in the middle Yangtze River valley produced one of the world's earliest pieces of evidence for rice domestication, with carbonized rice grains and rice leaf impressions found at the site. The Pengtoushan people were sedentary villagers who used red pottery and ground stone tools, practicing rice farming along with hunting and gathering. The Pengtoushan culture indicates that the Yangtze River valley was one of the key origins of rice agriculture in the world.

v.t: Construction of Jericho wall and tower
v.s: Around 8000 BCE, the inhabitants of Jericho built a stone defensive wall (about 2 meters thick) around their settlement and a round stone tower about 8.5 meters high with an internal staircase. This is the world's earliest known defensive urban fortification, showing that Pre-Pottery Neolithic A (PPNA) society had the capacity to mobilize large labor forces for public construction.

v.t: Domestication of cattle in the Near East
v.s: Around 8000 BCE, inhabitants of the ancient Near East (modern southeastern Turkey and western Iran) began domesticating the aurochs (Bos primigenius). DNA evidence suggests that all modern cattle descend from about 80 domesticated aurochs. Cattle provided meat, milk, and draft power in early farming societies, making plow-based agriculture possible. Cattle domestication was a major chapter in the agricultural revolution, profoundly changing human production methods and economic structures.

v.t: Early agricultural settlement in Cyprus
v.s: Around 7950 BCE, migrants from Anatolia and the Levant established early agricultural settlements on the island of Cyprus (e.g., Khirokitia). They grew barley and wheat, raised sheep, goats, and pigs, and used stone tools. Cyprus was one of the first large Mediterranean islands to be colonized, and its early settlement proves that Neolithic humans had considerable seafaring ability. The round stone houses of Khirokitia are a typical feature.

v.t: Flourishing of Çatalhöyük
v.s: Around 7400-6200 BCE, Çatalhöyük (modern Konya Province, Turkey) on the Anatolian plateau reached its peak, becoming one of the largest Neolithic settlements (about 13 hectares, peak population of 5000-8000). Houses were densely packed with no streets, accessed via rooftops. Residents practiced agriculture (wheat, barley), animal husbandry, and trade, using obsidian to make tools and mirrors. Murals and mother goddess figurines reflect rich religious life.

v.t: Jiahu culture inscribed symbols in China
v.s: Around 7000-5800 BCE, the Jiahu culture (modern Wuyang, Henan) in the middle Yellow River valley produced symbols inscribed on tortoise shells, bones, and stone tools. Seventeen inscribed symbols were found, some of which resemble later Shang oracle bone script. The Jiahu people cultivated rice, raised pigs, brewed wine (the earliest wine residue in the world), and used seven-hole bone flutes (the earliest known musical instruments). The Jiahu symbols are considered an important origin of Chinese writing.

v.t: Early agricultural settlement at Mehrgarh
v.s: Around 7000 BCE, an early agricultural settlement appeared at Mehrgarh in modern Balochistan, Pakistan. Residents grew barley and wheat, raised sheep, goats, and zebu cattle, and used stone sickles and querns. This site provides the earliest evidence of agricultural settlement in South Asia, laying the foundation for the later Indus Valley civilization.

v.t: Neolithic settlement at Hacılar
v.s: Around 7000 BCE, a Neolithic settlement formed at Hacılar (modern Burdur Province, Turkey) in southwestern Anatolia. Residents practiced agriculture and animal husbandry, using painted pottery and stone tools. Hacılar is famous for its early painted pottery with geometric and animal motifs. The site later flourished around 5700 BCE and is an important representative of Anatolian Neolithic culture.

v.t: First agricultural villages in Europe
v.s: Around 6500 BCE, the first farming populations entered Europe from Anatolia via the Balkans, establishing the earliest agricultural villages in southeastern Europe (Greece, Bulgaria, North Macedonia). They grew wheat, barley, and pulses, raised sheep, goats, and cattle, and used painted pottery and ground stone tools. This process, known as the Neolithic agricultural expansion, gradually spread across Europe over about 3000 years.

v.t: Early Jōmon pottery flourishes in Japan
v.s: Around 6500 BCE, the Jōmon culture of the Japanese archipelago entered its early phase, producing pottery with cord-marked impressions – one of the world's oldest pottery traditions. The Jōmon people lived by hunting, gathering, and fishing, dwelling in pit houses, and made elaborate pottery (especially flame-style vessels), showing a unique aesthetic and ritual tradition.

v.t: Doggerland land bridge submerged by North Sea
v.s: Around 6200 BCE, due to rising sea levels after the last ice age, the Doggerland land bridge connecting the British Isles to continental Europe was finally submerged by the North Sea. This area had been an important habitat for Mesolithic hunter-gatherers; archaeological finds include tools, bone artifacts, and human and animal footprints. The marine transgression forced thousands of inhabitants to migrate to higher ground, leaving oral memories of a great flood that may have inspired later flood myths.

v.t: Early Aboriginal Australian rock art
v.s: Around 6000 BCE, Aboriginal Australians were creating diverse rock art across the continent. In Arnhem Land's Ubirr and Kakadu regions, they painted naturalistic images of fish, kangaroos, and spirit figures on rock walls. Aboriginal Australians developed a complex 'Dreamtime' cosmology, a sophisticated seasonal resource management system, and oral history traditions. Aboriginal Australian culture is one of the longest continuously surviving in the world.

v.t: Rice domestication completed in southern China
v.s: Around 6000 BCE, the ancestors of the Yangtze River basin in China completed the domestication of rice (Oryza sativa). Large-scale carbonized rice grains were found at the Kuahuqiao and Hemudu sites in Zhejiang, indicating that rice agriculture was already quite mature at that time. Rice domestication is one of the most significant plant domestication events in the world, feeding more than half of the world's population. Rice cultivation later spread widely to Southeast Asia, South Asia, and East Asia.

v.t: Rise of Vinča culture and early metallurgy
v.s: Around 5700-4500 BCE, the Vinča culture flourished in the Balkans (modern Serbia, Romania, Bulgaria). The Vinča people are known for large settlements, densely planned houses, and long-distance trade. The earliest evidence of copper smelting in the world (c. 5000 BCE) was found at the Belovode site. The Vinča culture also created a set of inscribed symbols (Vinča symbols), considered the earliest attempt at writing in Europe. The Vinča culture had a profound influence on later Balkan and European Copper Age societies.

v.t: African Humid Period and origins of African pastoralism
v.s: Around 5600 BCE, the African Humid Period reached its peak. The Sahara region formed extensive grasslands, lakes, and river systems, and the inner Niger Delta expanded. North African populations developed cattle pastoralism, leaving numerous rock art scenes of cattle herding and rituals (e.g., Tassili n'Ajjer). After around 3500 BCE, the Sahara gradually dried, and populations migrated to the Nile Valley, contributing to the rise of Egyptian civilization.

v.t: Beginning of Sumerian Ubaid culture
v.s: Around 5500 BCE, the Ubaid culture began to form in southern Mesopotamia. The Sumerians established the earliest settled villages, developed irrigated agriculture (growing barley, wheat, dates), and began building small temples. The Ubaid culture was the precursor to Sumerian civilization, laying the material and cultural foundations for the rise of city-states in the later Uruk period. Ubaid painted pottery and temple architecture had a wide influence on surrounding regions.

v.t: Flourishing of Halaf culture
v.s: Around 5500 BCE, the Halaf culture in northern Mesopotamia reached its peak, renowned for its fine painted pottery decorated with complex geometric patterns and animal motifs using multi-colored glazes. Halaf people practiced agriculture and animal husbandry, lived in round mud-brick houses (tholoi), and established trade networks with surrounding regions.

v.t: Linear Pottery culture spreads agriculture to Central Europe
v.s: Around 5400-4900 BCE, the Linear Pottery Culture (LBK) expanded rapidly from the Danube basin along major European rivers (Danube, Rhine, Vistula), spreading the agricultural economy from central to western Europe. LBK farmers grew wheat, barley, and pulses, raised cattle, sheep, and pigs, and built long rectangular timber longhouses (up to 40 meters long). This was one of the largest population migrations and cultural diffusion events in European history, shaping the foundation of modern European population and language distribution.

v.t: Cucuteni-Trypillia culture mega-settlements
v.s: Around 5300-2750 BCE, the Cucuteni-Trypillia culture flourished in modern Romania, Moldova, and Ukraine. This culture built the largest Neolithic mega-settlements in Europe, with the Tal'yanky site covering 340 hectares and housing about 15,000 people. They constructed two-story clay houses, used fine painted pottery (spiral and geometric motifs), and practiced plow agriculture and animal husbandry. This culture pioneered quasi-urban prehistoric settlements in world history.

v.t: Fayum culture develops agriculture in Egypt
v.s: Around 5000-4700 BCE, the inhabitants of the Fayum region in the Nile Valley began organized agricultural production, growing wheat and barley and raising sheep, goats, and pigs. The Fayum culture used stone sickles, grinding stones, and produced woven baskets and pottery. These were the earliest agricultural pioneers of ancient Egyptian civilization, providing the economic foundation for the later unified pharaonic state.

v.t: Beginning of Yangshao culture Banpo phase in China
v.s: Around 5000 BCE, the Banpo phase of the Yangshao culture (modern Banpo village, Xi'an, Shaanxi) began to flourish in the middle Yellow River valley. Residents built a large settlement encircled by a ditch, divided into residential, pottery-making, and burial areas. Houses were semi-subterranean square or round structures, with defensive ditches dug around the perimeter. The Banpo people grew millet, raised pigs and dogs, and used painted pottery (the human-face fish basin is a typical artifact).

v.t: Domestication of maize in Mesoamerica
v.s: Around 5000 BCE, ancient inhabitants of Mesoamerica (modern Balsas River valley, Mexico) began domesticating wild teosinte, which after centuries of selective breeding became maize (corn). Maize became the staple food crop of Mesoamerican civilizations, supporting the rise of the Olmec, Maya, and Aztec civilizations. Maize cultivation subsequently spread north to North America and south to the Andean region of South America. Maize domestication was one of the most important events of the New World agricultural revolution.

v.t: Earliest copper smelting in the Balkans
v.s: Around 5000 BCE, the Vinča culture in the Balkans conducted the world's earliest known copper ore smelting at Belovode (modern Serbia) and Pločnik. Archaeological finds include copper axes, copper needles, and smelting furnace slag, with radiocarbon dates confirming c. 5000 BCE. This discovery pushes back human metallurgy by about 500 years and indicates that European technological innovation in the Copper Age predated that of the Near East.

v.t: Eridu becomes the earliest city in Sumer
v.s: Around 4500 BCE, Eridu in southern Mesopotamia developed into one of the world's earliest cities, with a population of about 5000. Eridu featured a large temple dedicated to the water god Enki, built on superimposed platforms that became the prototype for the later Mesopotamian ziggurat. Eridu is also the first city in the Sumerian King List where kingship is said to have descended from heaven.

v.t: Late Ubaid to early Uruk period in Mesopotamia
v.s: Around 4000-3500 BCE, southern Mesopotamia entered the Uruk period. The Sumerians developed early urban forms in settlements such as Eridu, Uruk, and Ur. Uruk covered about 50 hectares, becoming the largest settlement in Mesopotamia. Residents began constructing large temples, building complex irrigation canal systems, and creating clay tokens (the distant precursor of cuneiform writing) for accounting.

v.t: Naqada I (Amratian) period in Egypt
v.s: Around 4000-3500 BCE, the Naqada culture in Upper Egypt entered the Amratian period (Naqada I), with evident social stratification. Elite tombs contained painted pottery, copper tools, and cosmetic palettes, indicating the concentration of power. This culture prepared the social conditions for the later unification of pharaonic civilization.

v.t: Flourishing of painted pottery at Yangshao Miaodigou type
v.s: Around 4000-3500 BCE, the Miaodigou type of the Yangshao culture (Shan County, Henan) developed painted pottery characterized by petal and bird motifs. Its influence extended west to Gansu and Qinghai, east to eastern Henan, and north to the Great Wall region. The Miaodigou people built large public buildings in the center of settlements, possibly used for clan councils and ritual activities.

v.t: Mehrgarh period III in the Indus Valley
v.s: Around 4000 BCE, the site of Mehrgarh in Balochistan entered its third period. Residents began growing barley and wheat, raising zebu cattle, sheep, and goats. Numerous terracotta figurines and beads appeared, indicating mother goddess worship and craft specialization. This site provides the earliest evidence of agricultural civilization in South Asia.

v.t: Development of Buto culture in Egypt
v.s: Around 4000 BCE, the Buto culture site in the western Nile Delta showed different pottery styles and burial customs from the Naqada culture of Upper Egypt. The Delta region relied on marsh fishing, hunting, and small-scale agriculture, forming a decentralized tribal society that laid the groundwork for the later conflict between Upper and Lower Egypt.

v.t: Invention of the plow and the agricultural revolution
v.s: Around 4000 BCE, farmers in Mesopotamia and Europe invented the plow (ard/scratch plow), pulled by oxen to turn over soil. Plow agriculture was several times more efficient than hoeing, making large-scale farming possible and freeing labor for crafts and urban construction. The invention of the plow was the second major breakthrough of the agricultural revolution, directly promoting social division of labor, population growth, and the rise of early civilizations.

v.t: Domestication of potato and quinoa in the Andes
v.s: Around 4000 BCE, the ancient inhabitants of the Andes (modern Peru and Bolivia) domesticated the potato (Solanum tuberosum) and quinoa (Chenopodium quinoa). The potato was an ideal crop for high altitudes, with higher yields than grains. The potato later caused a population explosion after its introduction to Europe. Quinoa is rich in protein and was a staple food of the Incas. The domestication of these crops represents a major contribution of New World agriculture to world civilization.

v.t: Formation of early Sumerian city-states
v.s: Around 3800 BCE, the Sumerian city-states of southern Mesopotamia (Uruk, Ur, Lagash, Kish, etc.) began to form. Each city-state organized its economic life around a temple, with the priestly class controlling the redistribution of surplus. The Anu temple district at Uruk featured large public buildings, and conflicts over water and land began among city-states.

v.t: Beginning of Naqada II (Gerzean) period in Egypt
v.s: Around 3800 BCE, the Naqada culture entered the Gerzean period (Naqada II), with accelerated social complexity. Elite tombs contained more imported goods (e.g., bitumen from the Levant, lapis lazuli from Afghanistan). Pottery featured paintings with religious symbols, such as two-horned women and boat motifs, which may relate to the formation of kingship ideology.

v.t: Invention of wheeled transport in Sumer
v.s: Around 3800 BCE, archaeological evidence shows the appearance of solid wooden disc wheels in the Uruk cultural layers, initially used for four-wheeled oxcarts. The invention of the wheel greatly improved land transport efficiency and cargo capacity, promoting the expansion of trade networks among city-states.

v.t: Construction of ritual complex at Niuheliang, Hongshan culture
v.s: Around 3800-3000 BCE, the Hongshan culture in western Liaoning built a complex ritual center at Niuheliang (on the border of modern Jianping and Lingyuan counties), consisting of a goddess temple, stone burial mounds, and an altar. Large clay goddess statues and jade dragons were unearthed, indicating the emergence of a priestly elite and early social hierarchy. The Hongshan culture is considered one of the origins of Chinese ritual systems.

v.t: Appearance of copper knives in Majiayao culture, China
v.s: Around 3800 BCE, the Linjia site in Gansu yielded the earliest bronze knife in China (tin bronze), cast in a single mold, from the Majiayao culture. This indicates that the northwestern region entered the early Bronze Age and had cultural exchanges with the Central Asian steppe.

v.t: Introduction of donkeys as pack animals in Sumer
v.s: Around 3800 BCE, donkeys introduced from the Syria-Anatolia region were used by the Sumerians for long-distance trade transport. With their endurance and low feed requirements, donkeys made trade routes between Mesopotamia, the Levant, and the Iranian plateau more accessible, increasing the flow of luxury goods (lapis lazuli, timber).

v.t: Tell Brak becomes an early city-state in Syria
v.s: Around 3800 BCE, Tell Brak (northeastern Syria) in northern Mesopotamia developed into one of the world's earliest cities, covering about 110 hectares with a population of about 13,000. The site revealed large administrative buildings (the Eye Temple) and thousands of cylinder seals, proving it was an important administrative and trading center. Tell Brak flourished contemporaneously with Uruk, challenging the traditional view that the 'urban revolution' occurred only in Sumer and showing that northern Mesopotamia also experienced early urbanization.

v.t: Foundation of Ebla in Mesopotamia
v.s: Around 3600 BCE, settlement began at Ebla in northern Syria, which later developed into a trading hub connecting the Mediterranean and Mesopotamia. The city is famous for the discovery of about 20,000 cuneiform tablets documenting the economic, diplomatic, and legal activities of a powerful early Semitic kingdom.

v.t: Hierakonpolis becomes religious center of Upper Egypt
v.s: Around 3600 BCE, Hierakonpolis (modern Kom el-Ahmar) in Upper Egypt began building a temple to the god Horus and producing finely carved palettes. This city later served as the base of Narmer in the unification process, and the famous Narmer Palette was found there.

v.t: Formation of early settlement layer at Troy, Anatolia
v.s: Around 3600-3500 BCE, the first inhabitants appeared on the Hisarlik hill, building stone-and-wood houses and defensive walls. This was the initial village of Homeric Troy (Troy I), controlling the Dardanelles strait.

v.t: Early Elamite settlement at Susa, Iran
v.s: Around 3600 BCE, early Elamite settlements appeared at Susa, developing irrigated agriculture from the Zagros foothills to the Karun River plain. Susa's strategic location made it a transit point between Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley, later becoming the core of Elamite civilization.

v.t: Fortifications and early warfare evidence at Tell Hamoukar
v.s: Around 3600 BCE, large-scale fortifications and destruction layers from warfare were found at Tell Hamoukar (northeastern Syria). Excavations uncovered thousands of clay sling bullets (about 1200) and collapsed city walls, indicating that the city was systematically attacked and looted. This is one of the earliest pieces of evidence for siege warfare in Mesopotamia, showing that inter-city conflict and military violence appeared early in civilization.

v.t: Domestication of wild horses on the Eurasian steppe
v.s: Large quantities of horse bones and corral traces found at archaeological sites in Ukraine and Kazakhstan (e.g., Botai) prove that humans began domesticating wild horses around 3500 BCE. Horse domestication greatly changed human transportation, warfare, and agricultural production, enabling the formation of nomadic pastoralist cultures and facilitating exchanges between East and West across Eurasia.

v.t: Rise of Elamite civilization and the city of Susa
v.s: Around 3500 BCE, the Elamites in southwestern Iran established their earliest urban settlement at Susa. Susa became the political and religious center of Elamite civilization, developing a unique writing system (Proto-Elamite script) and artistic style. Elam engaged in trade, cultural exchange, and military confrontation with Sumer for a millennium. Elamite civilization was one of the earliest on the Iranian plateau and profoundly influenced the later Persian Empire.

v.t: Sahara desertification accelerates, population migrates to Nile Valley
v.s: Around 3500-2500 BCE, the Sahara region rapidly dried, turning grassland into desert. Inhabitants were forced to migrate to the Nile Valley, Lake Chad basin, and West Africa. Numerous rock paintings (e.g., Tassili n'Ajjer) document the transition from cattle herding to camel caravans during this period. The concentration of population in the Nile Valley directly drove the rise of ancient Egyptian civilization and the formation of a unified state.

v.t: Sumerians invent proto-cuneiform writing
v.s: Around 3400-3200 BCE (Uruk IV layer), priests and administrators at Uruk used sharpened reeds to incise signs on moist clay tablets to record the distribution of grain, livestock, and labor. These proto-cuneiform signs were initially pictographic and gradually evolved into abstract wedge-shaped signs. The earliest tablets found at Uruk contain about 2000 distinct signs. This is one of the earliest mature writing systems in the world.

v.t: Scorpion II (predecessor of Narmer) rules Upper Egypt
v.s: Around 3400 BCE, Scorpion II of Hierakonpolis ruled Upper Egypt and began wars to unify the Nile Valley. The Narmer Palette (actually belonging to the later Narmer) and the Scorpion macehead depict Scorpion presiding over irrigation ceremonies and conquering Lower Egyptian peoples. Scorpion II was the last important ruler of the Predynastic period, laying the foundation for Narmer's eventual unification of Upper and Lower Egypt.

v.t: Ritualized seal hunting in middle-late Norte Chico civilization, Peru
v.s: Around 3400 BCE, the Aspero site of the Norte Chico civilization on the northern Pacific coast of Peru produced ritual deposits of seals and fish. The inhabitants relied on marine resources to establish semi-sedentary life, building earthen mounds with stepped platforms, among the earliest public architecture in the Andes.

v.t: Bronze Age begins in the Near East
v.s: Around 3300 BCE, the ancient Near East entered the Bronze Age, with tin bronze (copper-tin alloy) replacing pure copper as the primary material for tools and weapons. Tin bronze is much harder than pure copper, enabling more efficient tools and sharper weapons. Bronze metallurgy spread from Mesopotamia to Anatolia, Iran, Egypt, and the Aegean region. The advent of the Bronze Age was one of the most important technological advances in the ancient world after the agricultural revolution, profoundly changing warfare, trade, and power structures.

v.t: Early administrative writing system in Mesopotamia
v.s: Around 3200 BCE, the late Uruk period of Sumer developed a systematic proto-cuneiform script for economic management. Thousands of clay tablets from Uruk record the distribution of grain, livestock, textiles, and labor. The script started as pictographic signs and later evolved into abstract wedge-shaped signs. The invention of writing enabled knowledge to be transmitted across time and space, promoted the complexity of social management, and was one of the most revolutionary innovations in human history.

v.t: Narmer (Menes) conquers Lower Egypt, establishes First Dynasty
v.s: Around 3100 BCE, King Narmer of Upper Egypt led his army down the Nile and defeated the forces of Lower Egypt in the Delta region, completing the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt. The Narmer Palette, found at Hierakonpolis, depicts him wearing both the Red Crown (Lower Egypt) and the White Crown (Upper Egypt) on its two sides, marking this historical event. Narmer founded his capital at Memphis (near modern Cairo) and established the First Dynasty, marking the formal beginning of pharaonic civilization.

v.t: Narmer establishes Horus name
v.s: Around 3100 BCE, Narmer wrote his name within a serekh (a rectangular frame representing the palace facade), above which stood the Horus falcon god. This Horus name became the core of all later pharaohs' royal titles, proclaiming that the pharaoh was the earthly incarnation of Horus.

v.t: Egyptian hieroglyphic writing system established
v.s: Around 3100 BCE, the Narmer Palette featured fully formed hieroglyphic inscriptions containing logographic, phonetic, and determinative signs. Scribes used papyrus rolls and reed pens to record administrative documents, religious texts, and historical events. This writing system was used for the next 3500 years, comprising about 700 common signs.

v.t: Narmer campaigns in Nubia, seizes quarries
v.s: Around 3100 BCE, Narmer led his army south up the Nile to near the Second Cataract, conquering local Nubian tribes and seizing important sources of schist, gold, and amethyst. These resources were used to make royal statues and sacred objects, supporting the divine nature of kingship.

v.t: Construction of White Wall palace and Ptah temple at Memphis
v.s: Around 3100 BCE, Pharaoh Narmer built a palace and fortification called White Wall (Ineb-hedj) on the west bank of the Nile, which later developed into the city of Memphis. At the same time, he built a temple for the craftsman god Ptah, who became the local deity of Memphis.

v.t: Pharaoh Hor-Aha succeeds Narmer
v.s: Around 3100-3050 BCE, Narmer's son Aha (meaning 'fighter') succeeded him, suppressing residual resistance in Lower Egypt. He established the nomarchy (provincial) administrative system in the Delta and introduced the 'Two Ladies' motif on the serekh, indicating that he enjoyed the protection of both tutelary goddesses (the vulture goddess of Upper Egypt and the cobra goddess of Lower Egypt).

v.t: Peak of late Uruk culture in Sumer
v.s: Around 3100 BCE, the city of Uruk covered about 250 hectares with an estimated population of 40,000-50,000, making it the largest city in the world at the time. The city wall was about 9 km long, enclosing the Anu temple district, the Eanna temple district, and large storage areas. Uruk culture spread cuneiform, cylinder seals, and metrological systems to Syria and western Iran through trade and colonization. The walls of Uruk mentioned in the Epic of Gilgamesh were built during this period.

v.t: Sumerians build city-state defensive systems
v.s: Around 3100 BCE, vying for water and farmland, Sumerian city-states (Uruk, Ur, Lagash, etc.) began to encircle their settlements with walls made of mudbrick. The appearance of city defenses marked the escalation of warfare from tribal conflict to formal war between city-states, promoting the development of military technology and professional soldiers.

v.t: Pharaoh Djer suppresses rebellion in Lower Egypt
v.s: Around 3050 BCE, after Aha's son Djer succeeded, rebellion broke out again in Lower Egypt. Djer brutally suppressed it in the Nile Delta and displayed the mummy of the rebel leader as a trophy. He built a huge step-tomb at Saqqara and established multiple human sacrificial burial pits, reflecting the tradition of human sacrifice in the early pharaonic period.

v.t: Egyptian expedition reaches the Land of Punt
v.s: Around 3050 BCE, Pharaoh Djer sent a fleet south along the Red Sea to the Land of Punt (modern Somalia or Eritrea coast). The fleet returned with myrrh, ebony, ivory, cinnamon, and live baboons, which were offered as tribute to the temples. This is one of the earliest recorded overseas expeditions in Egyptian history.

v.t: Construction of royal tombs at Umm el-Qa'ab, Abydos
v.s: Around 3050 BCE, large mudbrick tomb chambers began to be built at Umm el-Qa'ab in Abydos, Upper Egypt, with grave goods including ivory tags, stone vessels, and carnelian beads. This cemetery later became the royal necropolis for the 1st and 2nd Dynasties. Djer's tomb (designated O) is large, with hundreds of subsidiary burial pits.

v.t: Pharaoh Den first uses double crown, marking full unification of Egypt
v.s: Around 3000 BCE, Djer's son Den succeeded and, on an Abydos stela, was depicted wearing both the Red Crown and the White Crown for the first time, formally declaring the complete unification of Upper and Lower Egypt. During his reign, he strengthened border defenses in Nubia, established the office of Chancellor of Lower Egypt, and recorded the first complete Sed festival (a ritual renewal of the pharaoh's strength after 30 years of rule). Den's tomb yielded the first complete king list.

v.t: Pharaoh Den leads mining expedition to Sinai Peninsula
v.s: Around 3000 BCE, Den led an army of 10,000 across the Sinai desert to seize turquoise and copper mines (modern Serabit el-Khadim). He built a temple to the goddess Hathor there and erected a bronze statue of the pharaoh, marking direct Egyptian control over Sinai's resources.

v.t: Spread of papyrus paper-making technology in Egypt
v.s: Around 3000 BCE, scribes began systematically using paper made from pressed papyrus stalks, replacing bulky potsherds and stone slabs. Papyrus was easy to write on and transport, enabling the mass copying of administrative documents and literary works. The earliest unused papyrus rolls were found in a First Dynasty tomb.

v.t: Cycladic civilization flourishes in the Greek islands
v.s: Around 3000 BCE, the Early Bronze Age culture of the Cycladic islands reached its peak, producing numerous marble figurines (mostly female, with arms folded across the chest) widely used in trade and burial. Cycladic culture was one of the earliest Bronze Age cultures in the Aegean, maintaining close maritime links with the Minoan civilization and the Greek mainland.

v.t: Early Dynastic Period begins in Sumer (ED I)
v.s: Around 2900 BCE, Sumer entered the Early Dynastic Period I. The political systems of the city-states matured, with rulers holding different titles such as en (high priest), ensi (governor), and lugal (king). Kish became the hegemon in the north, and the early kings recorded in the Sumerian King List were often from Kish.

v.t: Pharaoh Qa'a restores order, ends First Dynasty
v.s: Around 2900 BCE, Qa'a reconsolidated central power, built a magnificent tomb (Abydos tomb Q), and ended the practice of human sacrifice, replacing it with substitute figurines. He was the last pharaoh of the First Dynasty, which lasted about 200 years with 8 pharaohs.

v.t: Hotepsekhemwy, founder of the Second Dynasty, succeeds
v.s: Around 2890 BCE, after Qa'a's death, power struggles ended the First Dynasty. Hotepsekhemwy founded the Second Dynasty. His name means 'the two powers are at peace', suggesting he unified the forces of Upper and Lower Egypt through compromise.

v.t: Emergence of eggshell black pottery in Longshan culture, China
v.s: Around 2900-2300 BCE, potters of the early Longshan culture in the lower Yellow River valley invented wheel-throwing and carburizing processes, producing eggshell black pottery only 1-2 mm thick, with a glossy black surface. These tall cups were primarily used in elite rituals, reflecting deepening social stratification.

v.t: Early urban rise of Harappan civilization in the Indus Valley
v.s: Around 2900 BCE, the Indus Valley entered the Early Harappan phase, with early urban precursors at sites such as Kot Diji and Amri. Residents grew barley and wheat, raised zebu cattle, used red-slipped black-painted pottery, and gradually expanded trade networks.

v.t: Pharaoh Peribsen adopts Seth, reflecting religious civil war
v.s: Around 2800 BCE, Second Dynasty Pharaoh Peribsen abandoned the traditional Horus name and adopted the god Seth (god of chaos and desert) as his patron, replacing the falcon on the serekh with the Seth animal. This likely reflects religious divisions and possibly civil war within Egypt, suggesting ongoing tensions between Upper and Lower Egypt.

v.t: Enmebaragesi of Kish in Sumer
v.s: Around 2800 BCE, King Enmebaragesi of Kish is the first historical figure in the Sumerian King List to have archaeological evidence. He conquered Elam and seized its weapons as booty, recorded on a fragment found at Nippur.

v.t: Troy II shows double walls and palace
v.s: Around 2800-2600 BCE, the second layer of Troy (Troy II) built a massive stone wall 300 meters long and a rectangular palace with a high-roofed great hall. Because of its wealth and fire destruction, Schliemann mistakenly identified this layer as Homeric Troy.

v.t: Construction of Yaoshan altar in Liangzhu culture, China
v.s: Around 2800 BCE, the Liangzhu culture (c. 3300-2300 BCE) built a stepped altar and elite cemetery at Yaoshan in Yuhang, Zhejiang. The altar likely served both observational and ritual functions, indicating that Liangzhu society had formed a unified religious belief and power system. Liangzhu culture is known for its developed rice agriculture, large-scale water conservancy projects, and exquisite jade artifacts, and is a key culture in exploring the origins of Chinese civilization.

v.t: Khasekhemwy reunifies Egypt, ends Second Dynasty
v.s: Around 2700 BCE, Khasekhemwy (meaning 'the two powers appear') reunified Upper and Lower Egypt, with inscriptions on his statue base at Hierakonpolis recording the killing of 48,205 northern enemies. He built a large stone tomb (Tomb V) at Abydos and placed many copper tools inside. He was the last pharaoh of the Second Dynasty and the direct precursor of the Third Dynasty.

v.t: Construction of pyramid platforms at Caral
v.s: Around 2700 BCE, the Norte Chico center of Caral began building large stepped pyramid platforms (the largest about 18 m high) in the arid valleys of Peru. Caral people based their society on cotton-fish exchange and developed a peaceful trade society with no fortifications. Caral is one of the oldest urban sites in the Americas, dating back to 3000 BCE.

v.t: Traditional date: Yellow Emperor ascends
v.s: According to retrospective calculation from China's Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji), around 2698 BCE, Xuanyuan (the Yellow Emperor) became the leader of a tribal alliance. The Yellow Emperor united with the Yan Emperor's tribe to defeat Chiyou at Zhuolu (modern Zhuolu, Hebei), and is credited with inventing boats, carts, clothing, and the south-pointing chariot, while his wife Leizu began sericulture. Although this date is a later calculation, it reflects the collective memory of the diverse origins of Chinese civilization.

v.t: Djoser establishes Third Dynasty, beginning of Old Kingdom
v.s: Around 2686 BCE, Djoser succeeded Khasekhemwy, moved the capital to Memphis, and built many stone structures. He appointed Imhotep as vizier and chief architect, who built the world's first large-scale stone building – the Step Pyramid at Saqqara. The Third Dynasty marks the beginning of the Old Kingdom, the age of the pyramids in Egypt.

v.t: Imhotep builds the Step Pyramid of Djoser
v.s: Around 2686-2648 BCE, Imhotep initially designed a traditional mastaba tomb for Djoser, but expanded it six times to become a six-stepped pyramid 62 meters high. This is the world's first large-scale stone structure, with an outer casing of limestone blocks and complex corridors and burial chambers inside. For his contributions, Imhotep was later deified as the god of wisdom and medicine.

v.t: Early Minoan civilization (Pre-palatial period) on Crete
v.s: Around 2700-2200 BCE, the Minoan civilization on the Aegean island of Crete entered the Early Bronze Age (Pre-palatial period). Palaces at Knossos, Phaistos, and Malia began to be built. The Minoans lived by maritime trade, with close ties to Egypt and Syria. They used Linear A script (undeciphered) and created exquisite frescoes, pottery, and gold and silver artifacts.

v.t: Early planning of Mohenjo-Daro in the Indus Valley
v.s: Around 2600 BCE, the Indus Valley civilization (Harappan) entered its mature phase. Mohenjo-Daro began to be built on a regular grid layout, using standardized fired bricks for streets and drainage systems. The city contained a raised citadel, the Great Bath, granaries, and assembly halls. The advanced planning of this city demonstrates a highly developed urban civilization and social organization.

v.t: Khufu succeeds Sneferu
v.s: Around 2589 BCE, Khufu (Greek Cheops) succeeded, moved the capital back to the Memphis region, and chose the Giza plateau as the site for his pyramid. He concentrated national resources to build his tomb. Under Sneferu, the Fourth Dynasty had completed the technological transition from step pyramid to true pyramid (the Bent Pyramid and Red Pyramid).

v.t: Construction begins on the Great Pyramid of Khufu
v.s: Around 2580 BCE, Khufu mobilized national labor (estimated 20,000-30,000 people year-round) to quarry bedrock and pile massive limestone blocks at Giza. The pyramid originally stood about 146.6 meters high, with a base length of 230.4 meters and an angle of 51°50'. Workers used wooden rollers, ramps, and levers to transport the stones. The project took about 20-30 years, using about 2.3 million blocks with a total weight of about 5.9 million tons.

v.t: Khafre builds the second pyramid at Giza
v.s: Around 2558 BCE, Khufu's son Khafre succeeded and built a pyramid at Giza about 143.5 meters high, with a base length of 215.5 meters and a slope of 53°. The pyramid retains part of its smooth limestone casing at the top. Khafre's pyramid is the best-preserved of the three great pyramids of Giza.

v.t: Carving of the Great Sphinx
v.s: Around 2550 BCE, Khafre used a natural limestone hill on the Giza plateau to carve the Great Sphinx, with a face about 5 meters long, 20 meters high, and 73.5 meters long. The statue faces east and likely represents Khafre himself, symbolizing the union of strength and wisdom. The Sphinx, together with Khafre's pyramid and valley temple, forms a complete architectural complex.

v.t: Royal tombs of the First Dynasty of Ur in Sumer
v.s: Around 2600-2400 BCE, the royal cemetery of the First Dynasty of Ur built large shaft tombs containing numerous gold and silver objects, lapis lazuli, copper weapons, and lyres. Dozens of sacrificial attendants and musicians were also buried in the tombs. The famous Standard of Ur is inlaid with scenes of war and peace, and the Lyres of Ur are decorated with bull heads and lapis lazuli inlay.

v.t: Funnelbeaker culture builds long barrows in Northern Europe
v.s: Around 2600 BCE, the Funnelbeaker culture (TRB) of Jutland and southern Scandinavia began building stone cists and earth-covered long barrows, similar in structure to the Newgrange tomb in Ireland. These are the earliest monumental burial structures in Northern Europe, used to bury tribal chiefs.

v.t: Fan Mountain burial yields jade cong king in Liangzhu culture, China
v.s: Around 2600-2500 BCE, the Fan Mountain burial M12 of the Liangzhu culture yielded about 6.5 kg of jade artifacts, including cong tubes, bi discs, and yue axes, carved with fine spirit faces (divine emblems). This reflects the Liangzhu culture's highly developed jade craftsmanship and unified religious belief system. The Fan Mountain cemetery is adjacent to the Mojiaoshan palace area, and the tomb owner should be the highest ruler of the Liangzhu ancient state.

v.t: Fifth Dynasty established, rise of sun god Ra worship
v.s: Around 2490 BCE, succession disputes at the end of the Fourth Dynasty led to the establishment of the Fifth Dynasty. Its founder Userkaf greatly strengthened the cult of Ra, building the first sun temple at Abusir. The size of pharaohs' pyramids decreased significantly, while sun temples gained independent status, and the power of the priesthood expanded.

v.t: Lagash-Umma border dispute in Mesopotamia
v.s: Around 2500 BCE, the neighboring city-states of Lagash and Umma began a dispute over irrigation water and land division that lasted for centuries. Lagash's Eannatum later erected the famous Stele of the Vultures to record his victory over Umma. The stele depicts vultures carrying off enemy heads and enemies caught in a net, and inscribed the first known peace treaty.

v.t: Harappan civilization establishes maritime trade with Mesopotamia
v.s: Around 2500 BCE, Harappan merchant ships sailed from ports in Dingba (modern Pakistan) via the Persian Gulf to Ur and Dilmun (Bahrain). Indus Valley seals and carnelian beads found in the Ur tombs confirm this sea trade route. Harappans exported cotton cloth, timber, and jewelry, and imported copper, tin, and lapis lazuli.

v.t: Ebla kingdom reaches peak prosperity
v.s: Around 2500 BCE, the kingdom of Ebla in Syria reached its zenith as a trade hub connecting the Mediterranean and Mesopotamia. Nearly 20,000 cuneiform tablets were unearthed, recording trade with Lebanon, Egypt, and Mari, as well as royal administrative orders and diplomatic treaties. Ebla was one of the earliest city-states in the Syrian region.

v.t: Kerma culture, precursor of Kush kingdom, rises in Nubia
v.s: Around 2500 BCE, the Kerma culture in the upper Nile (modern northern Sudan) became one of the earliest urban states in Africa. Kerma built huge mudbrick temples and fortifications (Deffufa), controlling trade routes for gold, ivory, and ebony into the African interior. Kerma had trade relations with Egypt during the Old Kingdom, later became a major rival of Egypt, and was eventually conquered by the New Kingdom of Egypt around 1500 BCE.

v.t: Lagash king Ur-Nanshe builds waterworks and temples
v.s: Around 2400 BCE, Lagash king Ur-Nanshe began large-scale irrigation and temple construction, leaving an inscription saying he brought timber from the sea to build the temple of Ningirsu. His son Akurgal succeeded him. Lagash became one of the richest Sumerian city-states during this period.

v.t: Fifth Dynasty Egyptian expedition to Punt
v.s: Around 2450 BCE, Fifth Dynasty pharaoh Sahure dispatched a large fleet via the Red Sea to Punt, returning with large quantities of spices, myrrh, and live giraffes. The expedition was depicted in detail on the walls of his pyramid at Abusir, showing the power of the Egyptian navy.

v.t: Reforms of Urukagina of Lagash
v.s: Around 2350 BCE, Lagash king Urukagina introduced social reforms, purging corrupt priests and officials, canceling debts of the poor, limiting oppression by the rich, and issuing one of the earliest legal texts. However, his city was soon conquered by his neighbor Lugalzagesi.

v.t: Sargon of Akkad unifies Mesopotamia
v.s: Around 2334 BCE, Sargon of Akkad first served as a courtier to the king of Kish, then staged a coup to establish the Akkadian Empire. He defeated Lugalzagesi, conquered Sumerian city-states, and campaigned in Elam, Syria, and Anatolia. He created the first standing army and unified administrative divisions, called himself 'king of the four quarters', and was the first ruler in history to establish a multi-ethnic empire.

v.t: Sargon founds new capital at Akkad
v.s: Around 2334 BCE, Sargon built a new capital, Akkad (location unknown), in the heart of Mesopotamia as the administrative center. He made Akkadian the official language, replacing Sumerian for writing cuneiform, making it the first Semitic writing system.

v.t: Sargon appoints his daughter Enheduanna as high priestess
v.s: Around 2330 BCE, Sargon appointed his daughter Enheduanna (c. 2285-2250 BCE) as high priestess of the moon god Nanna at Ur. She is the first named poet in history, composing many hymns to the goddess Inanna, pioneering signed literature. Her works were copied in Mesopotamia for over a thousand years.

v.t: Sargon defeats Elamite coalition
v.s: Around 2320 BCE, the Awan dynasty of Elam, together with Susa and Shimashki, rebelled. Sargon led his army east, defeated the coalition near Susa, and sacked the main Elamite cities. Thereafter Elam became a vassal of Akkad.

v.t: Naram-Sin succeeds and calls himself king of the four quarters
v.s: Around 2254 BCE, Manishtushu's son Naram-Sin became the most powerful ruler of Akkad. He completely suppressed all rebellions and deified himself. The famous Victory Stele of Naram-Sin depicts him climbing a mountain over his enemies; it is housed in the Louvre. Under Naram-Sin, the Akkadian Empire reached its greatest extent, from the Mediterranean to the Persian Gulf.

v.t: Naram-Sin conquers Magan
v.s: Around 2240 BCE, Naram-Sin sent an army south along the Persian Gulf to conquer Magan (modern Oman), thereby controlling Persian Gulf trade and copper resources.

v.t: Decline of Akkadian Empire, Gutian invasion
v.s: Around 2200 BCE, in the late years of Naram-Sin, the Gutians from the Zagros Mountains invaded Mesopotamia. The empire's borders contracted, and local governors became increasingly independent. After Naram-Sin's death, the empire quickly disintegrated. The Gutians ruled Mesopotamia for about a century, known as the Gutian dynasty.

v.t: Pepi II ascends at age 6 in Egypt
v.s: Around 2278 BCE, Pepi II ascended the throne at age 6 and lived to about 100, making him one of the longest-reigning monarchs in ancient history (about 94 years). By the end of his reign, the Old Kingdom had completely collapsed due to climate deterioration (declining Nile floods) and regional rebellions, leading to the disintegration of the state.

v.t: End of Old Kingdom, First Intermediate Period begins in Egypt
v.s: Around 2181 BCE, after Pepi II's death, Egypt entered the First Intermediate Period (7th-early 11th Dynasties). For several consecutive years, Nile floods were low, causing widespread famine. Thebes in the south and Heracleopolis in the north each established separate regimes, and civil war broke out between them.

v.t: Initial construction of Shimao site in China
v.s: Around 2300-1900 BCE, the Shimao site in northern Shaanxi, China, began building large stone gates, with walls incorporating rhombic timber-reinforced construction. Shimao covers about 4 square kilometers, making it the largest city of its time in China. Numerous jade artifacts were embedded in the walls. Its discovery shows that this region already possessed early state formation.

v.t: Construction of Taosi observatory in China
v.s: Around 2300-1900 BCE, the Taosi culture in the middle Yellow River valley built a semicircular rammed-earth observatory about 40 meters in diameter at Taosi, Xiangfen, Shanxi. It had 13 observation notches that could determine the solstices and agricultural seasons by watching the sunrise. The Taosi observatory is the earliest known field astronomical observatory.

v.t: Destruction of Ebla in Syria
v.s: Around 2200 BCE, the city of Ebla was destroyed, possibly by Naram-Sin of Akkad or by Hurrians. The fire burned the palace and the tablet library, and Ebla never recovered. The discovery of the Ebla archives provides invaluable first-hand material for studying the economy, diplomacy, and culture of ancient Syria.

v.t: Early Hittite kingdom forms in Anatolia
v.s: Around 2200 BCE, the Hittites established city-states at Kussara and Nesa in central Anatolia, using Old Assyrian cuneiform for records. King Anitta later conquered all of central Anatolia, leaving the earliest Hittite inscriptions. Hittite civilization was the earliest Indo-European civilization in Anatolia.

v.t: Mentuhotep II begins reunification wars in Egypt
v.s: Around 2130 BCE, Theban ruler Mentuhotep II succeeded, determined to end the division of the First Intermediate Period. During his reign, he reorganized the army and gradually pushed north, finally defeating the Heracleopolitan dynasty and reunifying Egypt, ushering in the Middle Kingdom. Mentuhotep II made Thebes his capital and built a cliff temple at Deir el-Bahari, elevating the god Amun from a local deity to a national god.

v.t: Middle Kingdom begins in Egypt
v.s: Around 2055 BCE, after Mentuhotep II unified Egypt, the Middle Kingdom (11th-12th Dynasties) formally began. Central authority was restored, pharaohs regained control of Nubian quarries and trade routes, and architecture and literature experienced a renaissance (e.g., The Story of Sinuhe). Pharaohs repaired irrigation systems and campaigned in Nubia as far as the Second Cataract.

v.t: Assyrian trading colonies established in Anatolia
v.s: Around 2100 BCE, Assyrian merchants established trading colonies at Kanesh (Kültepe) in Anatolia, transporting tin and textiles by donkey caravans in exchange for local gold and silver. Thousands of cuneiform tablets recording family affairs have been found, providing invaluable data for studying ancient Near Eastern trade and family life.

v.t: Ur-Nammu establishes the Third Dynasty of Ur
v.s: Around 2112 BCE, Ur-Nammu, governor of Ur, defeated Uruk and founded the Third Dynasty of Ur (Ur III). He issued the world's earliest known law code – the Code of Ur-Nammu – inscribed in cuneiform on clay tablets. It covered compensation for damages, marriage and family, slave management, etc., establishing the principle of monetary compensation over corporal punishment. It was about 300 years earlier and more humane than Hammurabi's Code. Ur-Nammu also built a massive three-tiered ziggurat (62 x 43 m at base) for the moon god Nanna in the center of Ur.

v.t: Shulgi succeeds to the Third Dynasty of Ur
v.s: Around 2094 BCE, Ur-Nammu was killed in battle (possibly attacked by Gutians), and his son Shulgi succeeded. Shulgi reigned for 48 years, bringing the Ur III dynasty to its zenith, with the empire stretching from the Persian Gulf to Syria. He standardized weights and measures throughout the realm, revised the calendar based on lunar cycles (12 months: 6 of 29 days, 6 of 30 days, total 354 days, with intercalary months), and established a standardized road system, postal stations, and a standing army. Shulgi deified himself in the 21st year of his reign.

v.t: Legendary Yao-Shun-Yu era in China
v.s: Around 2100 BCE (according to traditional chronology), China was in the legendary era of Yao, Shun, and Yu. The aging Emperor Yao selected the virtuous Shun as his successor, and the aging Shun passed the leadership to Yu, who had succeeded in flood control. This system of abdication was later regarded by Confucians as an ideal of political virtue. Yu passed his own door three times without entering while controlling the floods, finally succeeding in taming the Yellow River.

v.t: Early palace architecture of Minoan civilization (Old Palace period)
v.s: Around 1900 BCE, the Minoan civilization on Crete entered the Protopalatial period. Palace complexes at Knossos, Phaistos, and Malia were built. These palaces were arranged around a central court, with multiple stories, storage rooms, and water supply systems. The Minoans used Linear A script (undeciphered) for administration. The Minoan civilization was the earliest Bronze Age civilization in Europe and maintained active trade with Egypt and the Near East.

v.t: Establishment of the Xia Dynasty
v.s: According to the Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji) and the Xia-Shang-Zhou Chronology Project, around 2070 BCE, after Yu's death, his son Qi abolished the traditional abdication system and seized the throne by force, establishing the Xia Dynasty. This transformed Chinese history from 'public realm' to 'family realm', making the Xia the first hereditary dynasty in Chinese history. According to texts like the Bamboo Annals, the Xia Dynasty lasted for 14 generations and 17 kings, about 471 years. The existence of the Xia is associated archaeologically with the Erlitou culture (c. 1900-1500 BCE), but no direct written evidence has confirmed it.

v.t: Yu the Great tames the floods
v.s: Legend has it that during the time of Yao and Shun, a great flood occurred in the Yellow River basin. Gun failed to control it, but his son Yu switched to a method of dredging and channeling. After thirteen years, passing his own door three times without entering, he finally subdued the floods. Because of his flood-control achievements, Yu was chosen by Shun as his successor, eventually establishing the enfeoffment foundation of the Xia Dynasty. This event reflects the embryonic form of early state public works organization.

v.t: Yu meets the feudal lords at Mount Tu
v.s: After succeeding in flood control, Yu convened a meeting of feudal lords at Mount Tu (modern Huaiyuan, Anhui), received tributes from all quarters, and divided the land into the Nine Provinces. This meeting established Yu's hegemony among the lords and laid the political foundation for the establishment of the Xia Dynasty.

v.t: Yu dies, Qi succeeds
v.s: Yu died during an eastern inspection tour at Mount Kuaiji (modern Shaoxing, Zhejiang). His son Qi, supported by some lords, succeeded. Tribes such as the Youhu clan, dissatisfied, rose in rebellion. Qi's abandonment of the abdication system and establishment of himself marked the beginning of hereditary dynasty in China.

v.t: Rebellion of Youhu clan
v.s: After Qi abolished the abdication system and established himself, the Youhu clan (in modern Hu County, Shaanxi) rebelled, believing Qi had violated traditional rites. Qi led his army to fight the decisive battle at Gan (southwest of modern Luoyang, Henan), defeating and destroying the Youhu clan, thereby consolidating hereditary kingship.

v.t: Fall of the Third Dynasty of Ur
v.s: Around 2004 BCE, during the reign of the last king Ibbi-Sin, Amorites invaded and Elam declared independence. The city of Ur was sacked by the Elamite army, and Ibbi-Sin was captured and taken to Elam. The Third Dynasty of Ur fell, and Mesopotamia entered the Isin-Larsa period.

v.t: Establishment of the Isin dynasty
v.s: Around 2020 BCE, after the fall of the Ur III dynasty, Ishbi-Erra established an independent dynasty at Isin and called himself king of Sumer and Akkad. He recovered Nippur and gained control of the religious center of southern Mesopotamia, marking the beginning of the Isin-Larsa period. The city-state of Larsa became independent with Elamite support.

v.t: Taikang loses the throne
v.s: During the reign of Taikang, the second ruler of the Xia Dynasty (son of Qi), he was decadent, ignoring state affairs and often hunting north of the Luo River for months. Hou Yi, a leader of the Youqiong tribe from the eastern Yi peoples, took the opportunity to seize power, and Taikang was forced into exile. This event is known as 'Taikang Loses the Throne'. Xia rule was interrupted for about forty years, known as 'Hou Yi Replaces Xia'.

v.t: Harappan civilization in the Indus Valley begins to decline
v.s: Around 2000 BCE, due to river course changes, soil salinization, and trade disruptions, the Harappan civilization began to decline. Buildings at Mohenjo-Daro showed disrepair and slums, drainage systems were abandoned, and standardized weights, measures, and script fell out of use. By about 1700 BCE, the Indus Valley civilization had largely disappeared.

v.t: Expansion of the Isin dynasty
v.s: Around 2010 BCE, during the reign of Isin king Bur-Sin, the Isin dynasty controlled important cities such as Uruk and Lagash and reached a temporary peace with Larsa, consolidating its dominance over the southern two rivers region.

v.t: Larsa rises independently with Elamite support
v.s: Around 2001 BCE, Larsa king Naplanum consolidated his independence with Elamite support and began to expand in southern Sumer, competing with the Isin dynasty for hegemony in Mesopotamia. The rise of Larsa marked the formal formation of the Isin-Larsa period. Mesopotamia entered an era of more than two centuries of rivalry between Isin and Larsa.

v.t: Zhongkang and Xiang succeed, Hou Yi usurps power in Xia
v.s: After Taikang lost the throne, his younger brother Zhongkang was proclaimed king by some Xia nobles, but real power was controlled by Hou Yi. Zhongkang, unwilling to be a puppet, sent General Yin to attack Hou Yi's partisans but failed and died of grief. His son Xiang continued to flee, seeking refuge with his fellow vassals, the Zhenxun and Zhenguan clans. The central power of the Xia further declined.

v.t: Code of Lipit-Ishtar of Isin in Mesopotamia
v.s: Around 1930 BCE, Isin king Lipit-Ishtar issued a law code more detailed than the Code of Ur-Nammu, covering marriage, inheritance, and slave rights, written in Sumerian on clay tablets. This code is an important document for studying ancient West Asian legal evolution.

v.t: Decline of Eleventh Dynasty, establishment of Twelfth Dynasty in Egypt
v.s: Around 1985 BCE, during the reign of the last Eleventh Dynasty pharaoh Mentuhotep IV, Theban local power rose again and central finances dried up. After he was assassinated, the vizier Amenemhat I founded the Twelfth Dynasty, moved the capital to Itjtawy (near Faiyum), consolidated borders, and strengthened control over Nubia. The Twelfth Dynasty was the most prosperous period of the Middle Kingdom.
